narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong Year 1 grades placing you in the 8th decile—well above the typical student's 6th decile ranking—and solid Year 3 performance in the 6th decile, close to average. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 is slightly below the 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 33 falls short of the usual 37, and your family comes from a middle-income bracket, while you plan to attend part-time, which is uncommon as most students go full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 53% chance you'll fail the bar—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes those likely to pass on the first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your part-time enrollment plan, which stood out strongly since only about 7% of students choose this path and it often correlates with bar struggles in the model's view. On a positive note, your excellent Year 1 decile ranking was the biggest force pulling the prediction toward bar passage success, showing the model recognized your early academic promise as a key strength. Your Year 3 decile, while solid, had a milder positive influence, helping somewhat but not enough to offset the part-time concern. Compared to typical students who pass, your academics are competitive in places, but the part-time choice mirrored patterns in lower-success cases more than high achievers. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic student from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with adjustments. To move forward, consider reapplying full-time if feasible, as that alone could flip the model's outlook dramatically, or retake the LSAT to boost your score above average. Strengthening recent grades or exploring bar prep resources tailored for part-time students might also help in a future application. You're clearly capable, and with targeted changes, law school doors can reopen. Keep pushing—your Year 1 decile proves you've got the drive.
